title: Service Mesh Traffic Management with Istio
description: Learn how to configure advanced traffic management policies in Istio including weighted routing, automatic retries, and circuit breaker patterns for production services.

cards.front: What is an Istio VirtualService and why do you need one?
cards.back:
A VirtualService defines traffic routing rules for a service. Without one, Istio sends traffic round-robin to all pods behind a Kubernetes Service. With a VirtualService, you control how requests reach your workloads - split traffic by weight, match on headers, rewrite URLs, or route to specific versions.

apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: VirtualService
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  hosts:
  - my-app
  http:
  - route:
    - destination:
        host: my-app
        subset: v2

cards.front: What is an Istio DestinationRule and how does it relate to a VirtualService?
cards.back:
A DestinationRule defines policies applied to traffic after routing decisions are made by a VirtualService. It configures load balancing, connection pools, outlier detection, and TLS settings. It also defines subsets (named groups of pods selected by labels) that VirtualServices reference.

apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: DestinationRule
metadata:
  name: my-app
spec:
  host: my-app
  subsets:
  - name: v1
    labels:
      version: v1
  - name: v2
    labels:
      version: v2

cards.front: What is a subset in Istio and where do you define it?
cards.back: A subset is a named group of endpoints within a service, selected by pod labels. You define subsets inside a DestinationRule. A common pattern is labeling pods with 'version: v1' or 'version: v2' and creating subsets that match those labels. VirtualServices then reference these subset names to route traffic to specific versions.

cards.front: How do you configure a canary deployment in Istio that sends 10% of traffic to v2?
cards.back:
Use a VirtualService with weighted routing:

spec:
  hosts:
  - my-app
  http:
  - route:
    - destination:
        host: my-app
        subset: v1
      weight: 90
    - destination:
        host: my-app
        subset: v2
      weight: 10

Weights must add up to 100. You also need a DestinationRule that defines the v1 and v2 subsets with matching pod labels. Gradually increase the v2 weight as you gain confidence.

cards.front: How do you route requests to a specific service version based on an HTTP header?
cards.back:
Use a match block in a VirtualService:

http:
- match:
  - headers:
      x-test-user:
        exact: "true"
  route:
  - destination:
      host: my-app
      subset: v2
- route:
  - destination:
      host: my-app
      subset: v1

This sends requests with the header 'x-test-user: true' to v2, and everything else to v1. Useful for testing new versions with specific users or internal teams before a wider rollout.

cards.front: How do you configure automatic retries in Istio and what are sensible defaults?
cards.back:
Add a retries block to a VirtualService route:

http:
- route:
  - destination:
      host: my-app
  retries:
    attempts: 3
    perTryTimeout: 2s
    retryOn: 5xx,reset,connect-failure,retriable-4xx

Key settings:
- attempts: number of retry attempts (don't go above 3-5 for most services)
- perTryTimeout: timeout per attempt, not total
- retryOn: which conditions trigger a retry

Be careful: retries multiply load. 3 retries on a service handling 1000 rps can turn into 3000 rps during an outage.

cards.front: What does the retryOn field in Istio control, and what are the common values?
cards.back:
retryOn specifies which failure conditions trigger a retry. Common values (comma-separated):

- 5xx: any 500-level response
- reset: connection reset by the upstream
- connect-failure: failed to connect to upstream
- retriable-4xx: retryable 4xx codes (409 Conflict)
- gateway-error: 502, 503, or 504 responses
- refused-stream: upstream refused the stream (HTTP/2)

Never retry on non-idempotent requests (POST creating records) unless your service handles duplicates. Retrying a payment request could charge a customer twice.

cards.front: How do you set request timeouts in Istio, and why should you set them explicitly?
cards.back:
Add a timeout field to a VirtualService route:

http:
- route:
  - destination:
      host: my-app
  timeout: 5s

Istio's default timeout is 15 seconds - too long for most APIs. Explicit timeouts prevent slow upstream services from holding connections open and cascading failures across your mesh. Set timeouts shorter than the caller's timeout to fail fast. For example, if the frontend times out at 10s, set the backend VirtualService timeout to 5s.

cards.front: How do you configure a circuit breaker in Istio?
cards.back:
Circuit breakers are configured in a DestinationRule using connectionPool and outlierDetection:

spec:
  host: my-app
  trafficPolicy:
    connectionPool:
      tcp:
        maxConnections: 100
      http:
        h2UpgradePolicy: DEFAULT
        http1MaxPendingRequests: 100
        http2MaxRequests: 1000
    outlierDetection:
      consecutive5xxErrors: 5
      interval: 10s
      baseEjectionTime: 30s
      maxEjectionPercent: 50

connectionPool limits concurrent load. outlierDetection ejects unhealthy endpoints from the load balancing pool.

cards.front: What does outlierDetection do in an Istio DestinationRule?
cards.back:
outlierDetection watches individual endpoints (pods) for failures and temporarily removes bad ones from the load balancing pool.

Key fields:
- consecutive5xxErrors: how many 5xx errors before ejecting (default: 5)
- interval: how often to check for outliers (default: 10s)
- baseEjectionTime: how long to eject an endpoint (default: 30s, increases with each ejection)
- maxEjectionPercent: max % of endpoints that can be ejected at once (default: 10%)

Set maxEjectionPercent carefully. If set to 100% and all pods are failing, you'll eject everything and have zero capacity.

cards.front: What happens when Istio connection pool limits are reached?
cards.back:
When limits are hit, Istio returns an HTTP 503 (Service Unavailable) to the caller immediately instead of queuing the request. This is the "circuit open" behavior.

Key limits:
- maxConnections (TCP): max number of connections to the destination
- http1MaxPendingRequests: max queued requests waiting for a connection
- http2MaxRequests: max concurrent active requests

These limits protect your service from being overwhelmed. If your service can handle 100 concurrent requests, setting http2MaxRequests: 100 prevents a traffic spike from crashing it. The caller gets a fast 503 and can retry or show an error.

cards.front: How do you inject a delay fault in Istio to test timeout handling?
cards.back:
Add a fault block to a VirtualService:

http:
- fault:
    delay:
      percentage:
        value: 10
      fixedDelay: 5s
  route:
  - destination:
      host: my-app

This adds a 5-second delay to 10% of requests. Use this to verify that your timeouts, retries, and circuit breakers work correctly before they're needed in production. Always test with a small percentage first and never inject faults in production without feature flags or header-based targeting.

cards.front: How do you simulate HTTP errors with Istio fault injection?
cards.back:
Use an abort fault in a VirtualService:

http:
- fault:
    abort:
      percentage:
        value: 5
      httpStatus: 503
  route:
  - destination:
      host: my-app

This returns a 503 for 5% of requests without forwarding them to the service. Combine with header matching to target specific test traffic:

match:
- headers:
    x-fault-test:
      exact: "true"
fault:
  abort:
    percentage:
      value: 100
    httpStatus: 500

cards.front: What is traffic mirroring in Istio and when would you use it?
cards.back:
Traffic mirroring (shadowing) sends a copy of live traffic to a second service without affecting the primary request flow. The mirrored traffic is fire-and-forget - responses are discarded.

http:
- route:
  - destination:
      host: my-app
      subset: v1
  mirror:
    host: my-app
    subset: v2
  mirrorPercentage:
    value: 100

Use it to test a new version with real production traffic before routing actual users to it. Unlike canary deployments, mirroring has zero risk to users since the mirrored responses are thrown away.

cards.front: What is the difference between an Istio Gateway and a VirtualService?
cards.back:
A Gateway configures a load balancer at the edge of the mesh for incoming (or outgoing) traffic. It defines which ports, protocols, and hostnames to listen on.

A VirtualService defines what to do with that traffic - routing rules, retries, timeouts.

They work together: the Gateway accepts traffic, the VirtualService routes it.

apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: Gateway
metadata:
  name: my-gateway
spec:
  selector:
    istio: ingressgateway
  servers:
  - port:
      number: 80
      name: http
      protocol: HTTP
    hosts:
    - "app.example.com"

cards.front: Why can retries cause cascading failures, and how do you prevent it?
cards.back:
Retries amplify load. If service A retries 3 times to service B, and B retries 3 times to service C, a single user request can generate 9 requests to C. During an outage, this multiplier effect can turn a partial failure into a total meltdown.

Prevention strategies:
- Keep retry attempts low (2-3 max)
- Set perTryTimeout to fail fast
- Use circuit breakers alongside retries so you stop retrying once the destination is clearly down
- Only retry at one layer in the call chain, not every hop
- Use retry budgets (Istio doesn't natively support these, but keep total retries under 20% of baseline traffic as a rule of thumb)
